title: The Perceptron
caption: Frank Rosenblatt's Mark I Perceptron machine at the Cornell Aeronautical Laboratory, 1958.

overview.text: In 1958, Cornell psychologist Frank Rosenblatt built the Perceptron — the first machine that could learn to classify patterns by adjusting its own weights from examples. Implemented on the Mark I hardware, it could learn to distinguish simple visual shapes. The New York Times reported it as a machine that could 'walk, talk, see, write, reproduce itself and be conscious of its existence' — a degree of hype that would haunt the field for decades.

deep.text: A perceptron takes a weighted sum of binary inputs, passes it through a step function, and outputs a class label. If the output is wrong, each weight is nudged in proportion to the input and the error. This perceptron learning rule is guaranteed to converge to a correct solution if the classes are linearly separable — but only then.

challenge.prompt: In what year did Frank Rosenblatt introduce the Perceptron?

feedback.correct: Correct — Rosenblatt published 'The Perceptron: A Probabilistic Model for Information Storage and Organization in the Brain' in 1958.
feedback.incorrect: It was 1958. Rosenblatt unveiled the Perceptron at the Cornell Aeronautical Laboratory that year.

sources.title: The Perceptron: A Probabilistic Model for Information Storage and Organization in the Brain
sources.author: F. Rosenblatt
